name: Segmentation
description: Divide an object into independent parts, make an object easy to disassemble, or increase the degree of fragmentation or segmentation.
sub_principles: Divide an object into independent parts
Make an object easy to disassemble
Increase the degree of fragmentation or segmentation
examples: Modular furniture that can be rearranged in different configurations
Sectional sofa made of individual pieces

name: Taking out/Extraction
description: Separate an interfering part or property from an object, or single out the only necessary part or property of an object.
sub_principles: Separate an interfering part or property from an object
Single out the only necessary part or property of an object
examples: Use a loudspeaker separate from a noisy computer
Extract the heat from a solar panel and use it for water heating

name: Local quality
description: Change an object's structure from uniform to non-uniform, change an external environment or external influence from uniform to non-uniform, make each part of an object function in conditions most suitable for its operation, make each part of an object fulfill a different and useful function.
sub_principles: Change an object's structure from uniform to non-uniform
Make each part of an object function in conditions most suitable for its operation
Make each part of an object fulfill a different and useful function
examples: A pencil with an eraser on one end
Multi-zone climate control in a car

name: Asymmetry
description: Change the shape of an object from symmetrical to asymmetrical, or if an object is asymmetrical, increase its degree of asymmetry.
sub_principles: Change the shape of an object from symmetrical to asymmetrical
If an object is asymmetrical, increase its degree of asymmetry
examples: Asymmetric mixing vane to improve fluid mixing
Ergonomic handle shaped to fit the hand

name: Merging/Combining
description: Bring closer together or merge identical or similar objects, assemble identical or similar parts to perform parallel operations, or make operations contiguous or parallel.
sub_principles: Bring closer together or merge identical or similar objects or operations
Assemble identical or similar parts to perform parallel operations
Make operations contiguous or parallel; bring them together in time
examples: Multi-blade razors for a closer shave
Combining a phone, camera, and GPS into a smartphone

name: Universality
description: Make a part or object perform multiple functions; eliminate the need for other parts.
sub_principles: Make a part or object perform multiple functions
Eliminate the need for other parts
examples: A sofa that converts into a bed
Swiss army knife with multiple tools

name: Nested doll/Nesting
description: Place one object inside another; pass one part through a cavity in the other. Make one part pass through a cavity in the other. Telescoping.
sub_principles: Place one object inside another
Make one part pass through a cavity in the other
Use telescoping or retractable components
examples: Telescoping antenna that collapses for storage
Russian nesting dolls (matryoshka)

name: Anti-weight/Counterweight
description: To compensate for the weight of an object, merge it with other objects that provide lift. To compensate for the weight of an object, make it interact with the environment through aerodynamic or hydrodynamic forces.
sub_principles: Compensate for the weight of an object by merging with another object that provides lift
Compensate for weight by making it interact with the environment (aerodynamic, hydrodynamic, buoyancy forces)
examples: Helium balloons used to lift heavy objects
Hydrofoils that lift a boat hull out of the water

name: Preliminary anti-action
description: If it will be necessary to do an action with both harmful and useful effects, this action should be replaced with anti-actions to control harmful effects. Create beforehand stresses in an object that will oppose known undesirable working stresses later on.
sub_principles: Perform a counter-action in advance to prevent or neutralize harmful effects
Create preliminary stresses to oppose later undesirable stresses
examples: Pre-stressed concrete uses internal compression to resist later tensile loads
Immunization (introducing a weakened pathogen to prevent later disease)

name: Preliminary action
description: Perform, before it is needed, the required change of an object (either fully or partially). Pre-arrange objects such that they can come into action from the most convenient place and without losing time for their delivery.
sub_principles: Perform the required change of an object fully or partially in advance
Pre-arrange objects so they can come into action from the most convenient place without losing time
examples: Pre-pasted wallpaper that only needs water to activate adhesive
Pre-positioned emergency supplies in disaster-prone areas

name: Beforehand cushioning
description: Prepare emergency means beforehand to compensate for the relatively low reliability of an object.
sub_principles: Prepare emergency means beforehand to compensate for low reliability
Add safety margins or backup systems in advance
examples: Airbags in cars deployed during a collision
Parachutes for pilots and emergency backup power supplies

name: Equipotentiality
description: In a potential field, limit position changes so that the object need not be raised or lowered. Change the conditions of work so that the object need not be raised or lowered.
sub_principles: Change conditions so that an object does not need to be raised or lowered
Minimize potential energy changes during operation
examples: Spring-loaded platform that adjusts height as weight is added or removed
Canal locks that use water levels to move boats without lifting them

name: The other way round/Inversion
description: Invert the action used to solve the problem. Make movable parts fixed, and fixed parts movable. Turn the object or process upside down.
sub_principles: Invert the action or process
Make movable parts fixed, and fixed parts movable
Turn the object or process upside down
examples: Treadmill: instead of a person moving over ground, the ground moves under the person
Rotating the part instead of the tool in a lathe

name: Spheroidality/Curvature
description: Instead of using rectilinear parts, surfaces, or forms, use curvilinear ones; move from flat surfaces to spherical ones; use rollers, balls, spirals. Go from linear to rotary motion; use centrifugal forces.
sub_principles: Use curvilinear parts, surfaces, or forms instead of rectilinear ones
Move from flat surfaces to spherical ones; from parts shaped as a cube to ball shapes
Go from linear to rotary motion; use centrifugal forces
examples: Ball bearings replace flat sliding surfaces to reduce friction
Arched bridges distribute loads more effectively than flat ones

name: Dynamics
description: Allow or design the characteristics of an object, external environment, or process to change to be optimal or to find an optimal operating condition. Divide an object into parts capable of movement relative to each other. If an object is rigid, make it movable or adaptive.
sub_principles: Allow characteristics of an object or process to change to find optimal conditions
Divide an object into parts capable of movement relative to each other
If an object is rigid or inflexible, make it movable or adaptive
examples: Adjustable steering wheel that can change tilt and height
Flexible straw that bends to the desired angle

name: Partial or excessive actions
description: If 100 percent of an object is hard to achieve using a given solution method, then by using slightly less or slightly more of the same method, the problem may be considerably easier to solve.
sub_principles: If an exact amount is hard to achieve, use slightly less
If an exact amount is hard to achieve, use slightly more and then remove the excess
examples: Overfilling a casting mold and then trimming the excess
Spray painting a surface and then wiping off the excess

name: Another dimension
description: Move an object in two- or three-dimensional space. Use a multi-story arrangement instead of single-story. Tilt or reorient the object, lay it on its side. Use another side of a given area.
sub_principles: Move an object in two- or three-dimensional space
Use a multi-story arrangement instead of single-story
Tilt or reorient the object; lay it on its side
Use another side of a given area
examples: Multi-level parking garages instead of single-level lots
Spiral staircase to save floor space

name: Mechanical vibration
description: Cause an object to oscillate or vibrate. Increase its frequency. Use an object's resonant frequency. Use piezoelectric vibrators instead of mechanical ones. Use combined ultrasonic and electromagnetic field oscillations.
sub_principles: Cause an object to oscillate or vibrate
Increase the frequency of vibration, even to ultrasonic
Use an object's resonant frequency
Use piezoelectric vibrators instead of mechanical ones
examples: Ultrasonic cleaning uses high-frequency vibrations to remove contaminants
Vibrating concrete to remove air pockets during pouring

name: Periodic action
description: Instead of continuous action, use periodic or pulsating actions. If an action is already periodic, change the periodic magnitude or frequency. Use pauses between actions to perform a different action.
sub_principles: Instead of continuous action, use periodic or pulsating actions
If an action is already periodic, change the magnitude or frequency
Use pauses between impulses to perform a different action
examples: Pulsed laser cutting is more precise than continuous cutting
Interval training alternates high-intensity and rest periods

name: Continuity of useful action
description: Carry on work continuously; make all parts of an object work at full load, all the time. Eliminate all idle or intermittent actions or work.
sub_principles: Carry on work continuously; make all parts of an object work at full load all the time
Eliminate all idle or intermittent actions or work
examples: Continuous casting of steel instead of batch processing
Flywheel that stores energy during low-demand periods

name: Skipping/Rushing through
description: Conduct a process, or certain stages, at high speed (e.g., a harmful or hazardous process).
sub_principles: Conduct a process or certain stages at high speed
Skip over dangerous or harmful stages quickly
examples: Flash pasteurization heats and cools milk rapidly to minimize nutrient loss
High-speed cutting to prevent heat buildup in the workpiece

name: Blessing in disguise/Turn lemons into lemonade
description: Use harmful factors or effects to achieve a positive effect. Eliminate a harmful factor by combining it with another harmful factor. Amplify a harmful factor to such a degree that it is no longer harmful.
sub_principles: Use harmful factors to achieve a positive effect
Eliminate a harmful factor by combining it with another harmful factor
Amplify a harmful factor to such a degree that it is no longer harmful
examples: Using waste heat from a factory to heat nearby buildings
Sand blasting uses abrasive particles (harmful) for surface finishing (useful)

name: Feedback
description: Introduce feedback to improve a process or action. If feedback is already used, change its magnitude or influence in accordance with operating conditions.
sub_principles: Introduce feedback to improve a process or action
If feedback is already used, change its magnitude or influence
examples: Thermostat uses temperature feedback to control heating/cooling
Cruise control adjusts throttle based on speed feedback

name: Intermediary/Mediator
description: Use an intermediary carrier article or intermediary process. Merge one object temporarily with another (which can be easily removed).
sub_principles: Use an intermediary carrier article or process
Merge one object temporarily with another that can be easily removed
examples: Using a catalyst to facilitate a chemical reaction without being consumed
A carpenter's jig holds pieces in place during assembly

name: Self-service
description: Make an object serve itself by performing auxiliary helpful functions. Use waste resources, energy, or substances.
sub_principles: Make an object serve itself by performing auxiliary helpful functions
Use waste resources, energy, or substances
examples: Self-cleaning oven uses high temperature to burn off residue
A machine that sharpens its own cutting tool during idle time

name: Copying
description: Instead of an unavailable, expensive, or fragile object, use simpler and inexpensive copies. Replace an object with its optical copy (image). Use scale changes (enlarge or reduce).
sub_principles: Instead of an unavailable, expensive, or fragile object, use simpler and inexpensive copies
Replace an object or process with optical copies
If visible optical copies are used, move to infrared or ultraviolet copies
examples: Virtual reality simulation of a dangerous environment for training
Scale models for wind tunnel testing

name: Cheap short-living objects
description: Replace an inexpensive object with a multiple of inexpensive objects, compromising certain qualities (such as service life).
sub_principles: Replace an expensive object with multiple inexpensive objects
Compromise certain qualities such as service life
examples: Disposable diapers instead of reusable cloth diapers
Single-use medical instruments to avoid sterilization costs

name: Mechanics substitution
description: Replace a mechanical means with a sensory (optical, acoustic, taste or smell) means. Use electric, magnetic, and electromagnetic fields to interact with the object. Change from static to movable fields, from unstructured to structured. Use fields in conjunction with field-activated particles.
sub_principles: Replace a mechanical means with a sensory means (optical, acoustic, etc.)
Use electric, magnetic, and electromagnetic fields to interact with the object
Change from static to movable fields, from unstructured fields to those having structure
Use fields in conjunction with field-activated (ferromagnetic) particles
examples: Replacing mechanical switches with touch-sensitive controls
Magnetic levitation trains replace wheels with electromagnetic suspension

name: Pneumatics and hydraulics
description: Use gas and liquid parts of an object instead of solid parts (e.g., inflatable and filled, air cushion, hydrostatic, hydroreactive).
sub_principles: Use gas and liquid parts of an object instead of solid parts
Use inflatable, fluid-filled, air-cushion, hydrostatic, or hydroreactive structures
examples: Inflatable mattress replaces a rigid one for portability
Hydraulic jack uses fluid pressure instead of mechanical gears

name: Flexible shells and thin films
description: Use flexible shells and thin films instead of three-dimensional structures. Isolate the object from the external environment using flexible shells and thin films.
sub_principles: Use flexible shells and thin films instead of three-dimensional structures
Isolate the object from the external environment using flexible shells and thin films
examples: Shrink-wrap packaging protects products with minimal material
Greenhouse plastic film instead of glass panels

name: Porous materials
description: Make an object porous or add porous elements (inserts, coatings, etc.). If an object is already porous, use the pores to introduce a useful substance or function.
sub_principles: Make an object porous or add porous elements
If an object is already porous, use the pores to introduce a useful substance or function
examples: Porous brake pads absorb and dissipate heat more effectively
Sponge-like materials for controlled drug release

name: Color changes
description: Change the color of an object or its external environment. Change the transparency of an object or its external environment.
sub_principles: Change the color of an object or its external environment
Change the transparency of an object or its external environment
Use colored additives to observe objects or processes that are difficult to see
examples: Photochromic lenses that darken in sunlight
Adding dye to a leak detection fluid to find cracks

name: Homogeneity
description: Make objects interacting with a given object of the same material (or material with identical properties).
sub_principles: Make objects interacting with a given object of the same material
Make interacting parts from material with identical properties
examples: An ice container made of ice to store ice without melting
Diamond cutting tools used to cut diamonds

name: Discarding and recovering
description: Make portions of an object that have fulfilled their functions go away (discard by dissolving, evaporating, etc.) or modify them directly during operation. Restore consumable parts of an object directly in operation.
sub_principles: Make portions of an object that have fulfilled their functions go away
Restore consumable parts of an object directly in operation
examples: Dissolving surgical sutures that do not need removal
Rocket stages that separate and fall away after use

name: Parameter changes
description: Change an object's physical state (e.g., to a gas, liquid, or solid). Change the concentration or consistency. Change the degree of flexibility. Change the temperature.
sub_principles: Change an object's physical state (gas, liquid, solid)
Change the concentration or consistency
Change the degree of flexibility
Change the temperature or volume
examples: Freezing food for preservation and transport
Transporting natural gas as LNG (liquid) for efficiency

name: Phase transitions
description: Use phenomena occurring during phase transitions (e.g., volume changes, loss or absorption of heat, etc.).
sub_principles: Use phenomena occurring during phase transitions
Exploit volume changes during phase transitions
Exploit heat absorption or release during phase transitions
examples: Heat pipes use evaporation and condensation cycles for efficient heat transfer
Water expansion during freezing used for rock splitting in quarrying

name: Thermal expansion
description: Use thermal expansion (or contraction) of materials. If thermal expansion is being used, use multiple materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion.
sub_principles: Use thermal expansion or contraction of materials
Use multiple materials with different coefficients of thermal expansion
examples: Bimetallic strip in a thermostat bends with temperature change
Shrink-fitting: heating a metal collar to expand it, then cooling to tighten

name: Strong oxidants/Accelerated oxidation
description: Replace common air with oxygen-enriched air. Replace enriched air with pure oxygen. Expose air or oxygen to ionizing radiation. Use ionized oxygen (ozone). Replace ozonized (or ionized) oxygen with ozone.
sub_principles: Replace common air with oxygen-enriched air
Replace enriched air with pure oxygen
Expose air or oxygen to ionizing radiation
Use ionized oxygen (ozone)
examples: Oxygen-enriched air used in steel-making blast furnaces for higher temperatures
Ozone water treatment for purification

name: Inert atmosphere
description: Replace a normal environment with an inert one. Add neutral parts or inert additives to an object.
sub_principles: Replace a normal environment with an inert one
Add neutral parts or inert additives to an object
Carry out the process in a vacuum
examples: Welding in an argon atmosphere to prevent oxidation
Nitrogen-filled food packaging to extend shelf life

name: Composite materials
description: Change from uniform to composite (multiple) materials.
sub_principles: Change from uniform to composite (multiple) materials
Replace a homogeneous material with a composite one
examples: Carbon fiber reinforced plastic for lightweight strength in aircraft
Reinforced concrete combines steel rebar with concrete
